item.title: Apple introduces MacBook Pro with all-new M5 Pro and M5 Max
item.description: Apple announced the latest 14- and 16-inch MacBook Pro with the all-new M5 Pro and M5 Max.

item.title: XKCD Dependency
item.description: A web editor for p5.js, a JavaScript library with the goal of making coding accessible to artists, designers, educators, and beginners.

item.title: Open source package repositories face sustainability crisis
item.description: Opinion: Careless big-time users are treating FOSS repos like content delivery networks

item.title: Electric Vehicles
Giving LLMs a personality is just good engineering
item.description: AI skeptics often argue that current AI systems shouldn’t be so human-like. The idea - most recently expressed in this opinion piece by Nathan Beacom - is that language models should explicitly be tools, like calculators or search engines. Although they can pretend to be people, they shouldn’t, because it encourages users to overestimate AI capabilities and (at worst) slip into AI psychosis. Here’s a representative paragraph from the piece:

item.title: Welcome (back) to Macintosh
item.description: For at least 10 years, every Time Machine set up I have been in charge of, or tasked with maintaining for someone else, has eventually run into an issue where it stops backing up successfully. The only solution has been to start over, to not inherit backup state and to manually delete the old backup. My experience while looking up this solution repeatedly has been that this is not uncommon, and definitely is not just me holding it wrong; indeed the steps are marked as recommended solutions on Apple's own forums.

item.title: Is Code Still Relevant?
item.description: I've been writing code for a living for 16 years. The changes I've seen in the industry over the last year feel like something else entirely.

item.title: Postgres Column Naming
item.description: Where “?column?” comes from

item.title: Incentives Drive Everything
item.description: Incentives shape behavior. When value is invisible, proxies become targets, driving bloat, volatility, and rational dysfunction in modern orgs.
item.author: (https://www.facebook.com/yusufaytas)

item.title: Abstraction Beats Realism
item.description: How Abstract Visualizations of Audience Physiology Enhance VR Concert Experiences

item.title: Why Everyone Needs a Dead Man's Switch · Technical Blog · Alcazar Security
item.description: Why Everyone Needs a Dead Man's Switch

item.title: Dev Tools and Moats
item.description: Over the last week, I’ve had two different conversations with two different friends. One asked me where dev tools are headed. Another asked, almost casually, what happens to moats in an AI-first world.

item.title: Strix - First impressions
item.description: We’ve all heard it: penetration testers are over. Their job will soon be done by agentic AI frameworks that can find the same (or even more elusive) vulnerabilities for a fraction of their bloody money - and since they don’t need to sleep, eat, or have a work-life balance, they can run 24/7. And you, Red Teamers, are next. Ok, doomers, you got my attention. I decided to look at one of these rising AI penetration testing superstars, strix, and be generous enough to share my random thoughts with you. If you plan to test this tool yourself, check the APPENDIX: Practical tips for Strix testing section at the end of this post - I think I can save you some time and money. Here’s the TL;DR for those of you who don’t have enough time or patience to read my whole rant: After this test, am I scared to death and looking for a plumbing job? No, not yet. Am I impressed? Yes, I am. Actually, thinking about it, I’m very impressed.

item.title: How I built a sub-500ms latency voice agent from scratch | Nick Tikhonov
item.description: Nick Tikhonov's blog

item.title: Common Mobile UX Mistakes I See in Almost Every Project | Dinko Marinac
item.description: Subtle but frequent mobile UX issues that only become obvious when apps are built and shipped, covering dropdowns, modals, dashboards, and forms.

item.title: How to identify assumptions by distorting time – Abi Jones
item.description: Thinking tools: How to identify assumptions by distorting time

item.title: Zola is dead to me. Long live Kusachi!
item.description: I’d not long since switched from Carbon to Zola and added the external/ page to dork.dev (which at the time of writing this, still needs to be replaced on Kusachi). Here are some of my problems with Zola!

item.title: Do Not Write with an LLM
item.description: I have been seeing an increasingly prevalent trend of people showing up in online spaces flaunting that they are writing with the assistance of AI. They seem to be proud of this. They shouldn't be.

item.title: I Charge for a Single-File Web App I Can’t Protect From Piracy
item.description: By Joel Dare ⟡ Written March 1, 2026

item.title: So... can Home Assistant run DOOM?
item.description: It started with a DOOM t-shirt at a meetup. The real question? Can Home Assistant run DOOM? Two hours later, I had a fully working DOOM integration for Home Assistant and was fighting demons on my smart home dashboard.
item.author: (https://www.facebook.com/frenck)

item.title: No Code by Hand | Ashwini's blog
item.description: CI dropped from 37 minutes to 9, at 35% lower cost. Here is what we learned about the hidden queue, mixing Claude and Codex, and where the real multiplier came from.

item.title: Using Claude Code Agent Teams for Incident Investigation — magarcia
item.description: How I used Claude Code's agent teams to run a parallel, multi-agent production incident investigation — with one prompt and surprisingly good results.

item.title: Which programming languages pay the most? I made my own salary charts
item.description: Is Rust the best paying programming language? Or Julia? In this investigative article we will get to know the best paying languages, and much more.

item.title: Marketing Is Eating Engineering
item.description: AI is making every engineer dramatically more productive. As the bottleneck shifts from code to distribution, marketing and GTM roles are becoming the new moat.
item.author: (Eric Ciarla)

item.title: I let Claude improve my keyboard's firmware
item.description: A short story about how Claude improved my keyboard's firmware

item.title: The inner workings of TCP zero-copy
An identity crisis in engineering
item.description: Building software by hand was hard but pleasurable. When things didn’t work, we dug into the code and looked it up on the internet. We implemented the solution after we understood; we looked down our nose at the brute-force blind-pastes from StackOverflow. We drew enormous satisfaction from this. Of course, the clout we gained among coworkers for our ability to solve difficult issues didn’t hurt.

item.title: She Came Out of the Bathroom Naked, Employee Says
item.description: Bank details, sex and naked people who seem unaware they are being recorded. Behind Meta’s new smart glasses lies a hidden workforce, uneasy about peering into the most intimate parts of other people’s lives.
item.author: (Naipanoi Lepapa,Ahmed Abdigadir,Julia Lindblom,Erik Norman)

item.title: Repurposing Claude Code for Better Spotify Recommendations
item.description: I built a Claude Code skill that creates Spotify playlists from natural language. You describe what you want — “70s Ethiopian jazz fusion,” “ambient music that sounds like i…

item.title: Change your default date format to the least ambiguous
item.description: On 13th November 2025 a TikTok-er had beef with Air Canada.1

item.title: How I used Cursor to Migrate Frameworks
item.description: I upgraded kentcdodds.com from Remix v2 to React Router v7 in a day with over 17k lines of code changed. Here's how I did it.

item.title: Astro and Svelte: Why I Believe They're the Future of Web Development
item.description: Why Astro and Svelte represent a return to web development simplicity — backed by data from State of JS 2025, performance benchmarks, and real-world experience building this very website.
item.author: (https://xergioalex.com/about/)

item.title: I built a pint-sized Macintosh
item.description: To kick off MARCHintosh, I built this tiny pint-sized Macintosh with a Raspberry Pi Pico: This is not my own doing—I just assembled the parts to run Matt Evans' Pico Micro Mac firmware on a Raspberry Pi Pico (with an RP2040). The version I built outputs to a 640x480 VGA display at 60 Hz, and allows you to plug in a USB keyboard and mouse. Since the original Pico's RAM is fairly constrained, you get a maximum of 208 KB of RAM with this setup—which is 63% more RAM than you got on the original '128K' Macintosh!
